title: Enhanced localization length in a disordered one-dimensional band via cavity coupling to delocalized states
authors: Francesco Mattiotti, Guido Pupillo, Jérôme Dubail, David Hagenmüller
abstract: We investigate the localization properties of cavity-coupled electronic states in disordered systems, motivated by recent proposals of cavity-mediated hopping in quantum Hall systems. We first introduce a minimal two-band model in which localized states in a disordered one-dimensional band are coupled, through a homogeneous cavity mode, to an excited band of delocalized states. Combining perturbation theory with a transfer-matrix approach, we show that cavity-assisted hopping between localized states decays exponentially with distance, implying that the eigenstates remain localized even beyond the perturbative regime. Nevertheless, the corresponding localization length increases with the light-matter coupling strength and can extend over several lattice sites in the single-electron ultrastrong-coupling regime. We then study a disordered Landau band coupled to a cavity mode within the framework developed in Refs.[1,2]. We find that the effective cavity-mediated coupling between edge states also decays exponentially with distance, but with a localization length that can reach micrometer scales for experimentally realistic parameters. By analyzing the inverse participation ratio, we show that this enhanced coupling is predominantly mediated by the most extended states of the upper Landau band. Our results demonstrate that, while cavity-induced hopping in disordered quantum Hall systems remains exponentially localized, the associated localization length can become sufficiently large for the corresponding states to exhibit effectively delocalized behavior on mesoscopic length scales.
summary: This paper analyzes the localization of electronic states in disordered systems coupled to a single optical cavity mode. The authors demonstrate that while coupling induces hopping, this process remains exponentially localized. Crucially, they show that increasing the light-matter coupling strength enhances the localization length, leading to effectively delocalized behavior on mesoscopic scales.
main_problem: The study investigates how cavity coupling modifies the localization properties of electronic states within disordered one-dimensional bands, particularly in contexts like Quantum Hall systems.
main_result: Although cavity-mediated hopping remains exponentially localized, the associated localization length can be significantly enhanced by increasing the light-matter coupling strength, potentially reaching mesoscopic scales.
method: The analysis employs a combination of second-order perturbation theory and the transfer-matrix approach to determine spectral properties and localization lengths in the presence of disorder and cavity coupling.
model_or_system: The model is a two-band system: a disordered lower band (localized states) coupled via a homogeneous cavity mode ($\omega$) to an excited, delocalized upper band. This setup is applied both generally and specifically to disordered Landau bands in Quantum Hall systems.
key_observables: Localization length ($\xi$), effective coupling strength ($\Gamma_{f,j}$), Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR).
important_parameters: Light–matter coupling strength ($\Omega$), disorder strength ($W$), and the ratio of $\Omega$ to the spectral gap ($\Delta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis progresses from perturbative regimes to the single-electron ultrastrong-coupling regime, using approximations like the transfer-matrix method for large systems.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate the two-band model and show how effective coupling decays exponentially with distance, while also visualizing contributions to inter-edge coupling via IPR analysis.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes a minimal two-band model using perturbation theory. It then extends the analysis using the transfer-matrix method to study localization across various coupling regimes, culminating in specific applications to disordered Landau bands.
why_interesting: This work bridges concepts from cavity QED (light-matter interaction) with condensed matter physics (disorder/localization), showing how external fields can dramatically alter transport properties on mesoscopic scales.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the two-band model. A disordered lower band of localized states (energies ϵg,j, width W) is coupled via a single-mode cavity to a delocalized upper band with hopping J. Light–matter interaction induces virtual transitions between bands: counter-rotating terms couple even- to odd-photon sectors (e.g., |g, j⟩⊗|0⟩↔|e, j⟩⊗|1⟩), while co-rotating terms couple odd- to even-photon sectors (e.g., |e, j⟩⊗|1⟩↔|g, j⟩⊗|2⟩).

FIG. 2. Cavity-mediated coupling in the perturbative regime. Perturbative cavity-mediated coupling between the initial state |g, j⟩ and the final state |g, f⟩, both in the disordered band (logarithmic scale). The coupling is evaluated using Eq. (3) [displayed as the 2D colormap in (a) and blue markers in (b)] and compared with the approximate analytical expression of Eq. (10) [red dashed curve in (b)]. Parameters: ϵg,j ∈[−0.5, 0.5] uniformly random distributed, J = 0.1, Ω= 0.01, ω + ϵe = 3, and L = 50.

FIG. 3. Enhancement of the localization length in the two-band model. (a) Energy dispersion of the two-band model coupled to the cavity in the single-electron sector as a function of the coupling strength Ωfor system length L = 10 and keeping m = 20 bosonic Fock states; the inset shows a zoomed-in region. The colorbar indicates the weight of the eigenstates on the lower band (with any photon number). (b) Localization length ξ(E), obtained via a transfer-matrix calculation, as a function of the system size L for several coupling strengths Ωand fixed m = 20. For each value of Ω= 0.007, 0.5, 1, 3, 10, the energy is chosen at the center of the lowest band, namely E = −2.3 × 10−5, −0.12, −0.58,...

FIG. 4. Exponentially-decaying hopping in a disordered Landau band. (a) Normalized effective coupling Eq. (17) between an eigenstate |ϕn,λL⟩on the left edge and all the other eigenstates |ϕn,λ′⟩for the Landau band Hamiltonian, plotted as a function of the average position ⟨ϕn,λ′| x |ϕn,λ′⟩of each eigenstate. Thick dashed line: exponential decay of the coupling (guide for the eye). Thin dashed lines: average positions of |ϕn,λL⟩on the left edge, a representative state |ϕn,λR⟩on the right edge, and the corresponding normalized coupling. (b) Contributions of each eigenstate |ϕn+1,µ⟩to the normalized effective coupling between |ϕn,λL⟩(on the left edge) and |ϕn,λR⟩(on the right edge), shown...
